title: BCS-BEC crossover driven by small Fermi pockets of a high-Tc cuprate superconductor
authors: Junhyeok Jeong, Yamato Enomoto, Yoshimitsu Kohama, Tomotaka Nakayama, Kotaro Ando, Kifu Kurokawa, Soonsang Huh, Zhuo Yang, Toshihiro Nomura, Matthew D. Watson, Timur K. Kim, Cephise Cacho, Chun Lin, Makoto Hashimoto, Donghui Lu, Shiro Sakai, Takami Tohyama, Kazuyasu Tokiwa, Takeshi Kondo
abstract: Fermi arcs observed in underdoped cuprates have sparked debate over whether they represent segments of a large Fermi surface or small Fermi pockets. This ambiguity has long hindered their classification as either the conventional Bardeen-Cooper-Schrieffer (BCS) regime or the strongly coupled Bose-Einstein condensation (BEC) crossover limit. Here, using angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy and quantum oscillations, we demonstrate the coexistence of a small Fermi pocket and a large superconducting gap in the clean inner CuO2 layers of the four-layer cuprate Ba2Ca3Cu4O8(F,O)2. This coexistence constitutes a hallmark of the BCS-BEC crossover and has remained elusive for decades. Despite the presence of antiferromagnetic (AF) order, the superconducting gap in the small pocket is remarkably large, yielding a gap-to-Fermi-energy ratio (Delta_pocket/e_F ~ 0.6) and a critical-to-Fermi-temperature ratio (Tc/TF ~ 1.3) that reach the theoretical upper bound for two-dimensional superconductivity. Unexpectedly, this BCS-BEC crossover emerges not as the carrier density decreases but as it increases, abruptly within a narrow doping range of less than 1%. These results provide a long-sought microscopic foundation for the d-wave pairing mechanism in doped AF-Mott insulators.
summary: The study uses ARPES and quantum oscillations on cuprate superconductors to investigate the BCS-BEC crossover. By focusing on small Fermi pockets within the inner $	ext{CuO}_2$ layers, the authors demonstrate a large superconducting gap indicative of strong coupling. This work provides crucial evidence for the pairing mechanism in high-$T_c$ materials by showing that the pocket state dictates superconductivity.
main_problem: The study aims to resolve the ambiguity regarding Fermi arcs in cuprates—whether they represent large Fermi surface segments or small Fermi pockets—and to establish a microscopic foundation for the BCS-BEC crossover mechanism.
main_result: The authors demonstrated the coexistence of a small Fermi pocket and a large superconducting gap, providing evidence for the BCS-BEC crossover. Crucially, this crossover emerges upon *increasing* carrier density in the inner $	ext{CuO}_2$ layers.
method: The research combines Angle-Resolved Photoemission Spectroscopy (ARPES) to map band structures and measure gaps, with quantum oscillation measurements (dHvA effect) to determine Fermi surface geometry and effective masses.
model_or_system: The physical system is the four-layer cuprate superconductor $	ext{Ba}_2	ext{Ca}_3	ext{Cu}_4	ext{O}_{8}(	ext{F},	ext{O})_2$. The focus is on distinguishing electronic behavior between the inner and outer $	ext{CuO}_2$ planes, which exhibit distinct superconducting properties.
key_observables: Superconducting gap ($\Delta$), Fermi pocket size/location, ratio $\Delta_{	ext{pocket}}/\epsilon_F$, $T_c/T_F$ ratio, and the presence of a Bogoliubov flat band.
important_parameters: Small doping range ($<1\%$) where crossover occurs; high ratios like $\Delta_{	ext{pocket}}/\epsilon_F \sim 0.6$; and comparisons to theoretical upper bounds for 2D superconductivity.
main_assumptions: The superconducting properties of the inner $	ext{CuO}_2$ layers (IPs) are assumed to dominate or determine the bulk critical temperature ($T_c$).
figures_summary: Figures show Fermi surface mappings, ARPES spectra illustrating gap openings and band dispersions, dHvA oscillation plots confirming pocket geometry, and comparisons of superconducting gaps between IP and OP.
paper_structure: The paper systematically uses multiple experimental techniques (ARPES, quantum oscillations) on multilayer cuprates to characterize the electronic structure. It contrasts findings from different planes (IP vs. OP) and doping levels to build a cohesive argument supporting the BCS-BEC crossover driven by small Fermi pockets.
why_interesting: This work provides direct spectroscopic evidence of strong coupling physics in a highly correlated material, linking macroscopic superconducting properties ($T_c$) to microscopic electronic features (small pockets) near theoretical limits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
